metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Spoken Language 11 develops students' abilities to communicate effectively through oral and performative modes. Students study the craft of spoken language — including rhetoric, storytelling, debate, performance, and public discourse — and examine how spoken language shapes identity, community, and power. The course connects oral traditions and Indigenous storytelling practices as rich, legitimate forms of knowledge and expression. Students develop confidence, precision, and artistry as speakers and critical listeners.
metadata.general_description: A course developing oral communication, rhetoric, storytelling, performance, and critical listening skills — exploring how spoken language shapes identity, community, and power.

big_ideas.statement: Spoken language shapes our understanding of ourselves and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students examine how oral communication — from intimate conversation to public address — constructs meaning, builds identity, and shapes social realities.

big_ideas.statement: Effective speakers adapt their language, tone, and approach to purpose and audience.
big_ideas.elaboration: Grade 11 students develop the ability to analyze rhetorical situations and make deliberate choices about language, structure, and delivery to communicate with precision and impact.

big_ideas.statement: Oral traditions are sophisticated systems of knowledge, culture, and community.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students engage with Indigenous and global oral traditions as complex, valued forms of communication that carry cultural memory, values, and ways of knowing.

big_ideas.statement: Critical listening is as important as speaking in effective communication.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop active, empathetic, and analytical listening skills — recognizing that understanding others is foundational to productive dialogue and community.

competencies.raw: Apply appropriate strategies to comprehend complex oral texts; analyze the craft and structure of spoken language; recognize how oral texts reflect and shape perspectives and identities; engage with Indigenous oral traditions as sophisticated systems of knowledge.
i_can_statements: I can apply active listening strategies to comprehend and analyze complex oral texts — speeches, stories, debates, and performances.
I can identify and explain how rhetorical devices, structure, and delivery choices shape meaning in spoken language.
I can recognize how oral texts reflect and construct cultural values, power, and identity.
I can engage respectfully with Indigenous oral traditions and understand their role as primary knowledge systems.

competencies.raw: Create and deliver effective oral texts for a range of purposes and audiences; use rhetorical strategies, language, and delivery techniques purposefully; develop personal voice and style; collaborate respectfully in oral communication contexts.
i_can_statements: I can create and deliver polished oral presentations, speeches, and performances tailored to specific purposes and audiences.
I can use rhetorical strategies — ethos, pathos, logos — and stylistic choices purposefully to achieve communicative goals.
I can develop and refine my personal voice and speaking style across different oral contexts.
I can collaborate effectively in discussions, debates, and group oral communication tasks.
I can apply feedback to improve my oral communication craft.

areas.name: Rhetoric and Public Speaking
topics: Rhetorical triangle: ethos, pathos, logos — analyzing and applying persuasive appeals
Speech structure: introduction, body, conclusion; thesis, evidence, and call to action
Delivery techniques: voice projection, pacing, eye contact, gesture, and stage presence
Public address genres: informative speech, persuasive speech, tribute, and ceremonial address
Analyzing famous speeches: historical and contemporary examples of effective public address
Argumentation and rebuttal: constructing, defending, and challenging arguments in spoken form

areas.name: Storytelling and Oral Narrative
areas.raw: Oral narrative traditions and storytelling craft.
topics: Story structure in oral narrative: exposition, rising action, climax, resolution — adapted for oral telling
Personal narrative: crafting and performing personal stories with purpose and emotional resonance
Indigenous oral traditions: protocols, structure, and purposes of First Nations storytelling in BC
Global oral traditions: myths, fables, epics, and community narratives across cultures
Oral history: collecting, interpreting, and sharing community stories and intergenerational knowledge
Digital storytelling: podcasting, spoken word recordings, and audio narratives

areas.name: Debate and Dialogue
topics: Formal debate: parliamentary and competitive formats, roles, and procedures
Argument construction: claim, evidence, warrant — building logical and compelling arguments
Counter-argument and rebuttal: responding to opposing views with accuracy and respect
Socratic seminar: facilitating and participating in deep, text-based discussion
Restorative and Indigenous dialogue circles: consensus-building and community-focused dialogue
Mediation and conflict resolution through language

areas.name: Performance and Spoken Word
areas.raw: Performance, spoken word poetry, and oral artistic expression.
topics: Spoken word poetry: slam poetry, performance poetry, and voice as artistic instrument
Dramatic monologue: character voice, subtext, and theatrical delivery
Choral speaking: ensemble oral performance and voice blending
Oral interpretation of text: bringing written literature to life through the spoken voice
Performance preparation: rehearsal processes, feedback integration, and stage management
Connecting performance to identity and community

areas.name: Critical Listening
topics: Active listening: full presence, non-verbal acknowledgment, and paraphrasing for comprehension
Analytical listening: evaluating logic, evidence, and rhetorical strategies in spoken texts
Empathetic listening: listening to understand others' experiences and perspectives
Identifying bias, manipulation, and propaganda in spoken media
Listening in digital environments: podcasts, interviews, and audio media
Giving and receiving constructive oral feedback
